narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their grades, test scores, and backgrounds—to predict if someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 61% chance that you would not, which crossed the threshold for denial. Your profile includes a solid sixth-decile ranking in your first-year school grades, a fourth-decile in your third year, an upper-middle family income, a full-time study plan, an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, and an LSAT score of 32—credentials that reflect your hard work but also highlight areas where the model saw risks compared to others. The two biggest factors pushing the prediction toward bar failure were your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA, both of which fell noticeably below the typical student's levels of around 37 and 3.2, respectively, signaling to the model a higher struggle ahead on the bar. On a brighter note, your first-year decile ranking, which was stronger than average, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success, showing early promise in your academic performance. Your third-year decile, family income, and full-time status each nudged things slightly toward failure risk, though they played smaller roles overall—your third-year rank dipped below the norm, while upper-middle income and full-time plans are common but didn't offset the key gaps. Compared to typical students who pass, your LSAT and GPA stood out as the main departures, as those are strong predictors in most cases, while your first-year strength is more like what successful applicants often have. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or recommendations, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. Many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort, and law schools value resilience. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback or appeal options, and keep pushing—you've got this.
